doc_impression:
1. Part-solid nodule in the right upper lobe. Nonsolid nodule in the right lower lobe. Solid nodule in the right lower lobe. No change compared with the prior study. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, for both low- and high-risk patients, consider chest computed tomography in 18–24 months.

2. Centrilobular foci and bronchocele in the right middle lobe, consistent with bronchiolitis. The findings can be consistent with an infectious process (differentiate versus mycobacterial infection).

3. Splenic lesion, likely a cyst. No follow-up is required per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on Computed Tomography: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee, 2017.

4. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

In the lungs, solitary solid nodules (unchanged in number and size), in series 10008 presented as follows:
Right lower lobe:
* Nodule up to 4 mm on image 162.
* Nodule up to 4 mm on image 185.
Calcified granuloma up to 4 mm on image 179.

Left upper lobe:

* Micronodule 2 mm on image 86, perifissural.
No new areas of consolidation are identified.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy are identified.

Hypodense lesions in the right hepatic lobe, with interval appearance of new lesions with indistinct margins. doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Multiple peribronchial subsolid nodules are present in both lungs, 2–5 mm, forming a tree-in-bud pattern (more pronounced in the right lower lobe and in the left upper lobe).

Also in the parenchyma of the left lung, solitary solid nodules are present:
* A nodule in the upper lobe measuring 1.0 × 8 mm on image 81 (series 6).
* A nodule in the upper lobe measuring 5 mm along the minor interlobar fissure on image 106 (series 6).

The trachea and central bronchi are patent. The bronchial walls are thickened. Deformity and dilatation of most subsegmental bronchi and their branches are present, with cylindrical bronchiectasis, more pronounced in the right middle lobe (with decreased right middle lobe volume) and in the left lower lobe.

Areas of linear and focal pulmonary fibrosis are present with formation of traction bronchiectasis perifissural bilaterally.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass is identified. No lymphadenopathy is identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Wall calcification is present. The pulmonary trunk is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with sclerosis of the subchondral portions of the vertebral endplates. Decreased attenuation of vertebrae T12 and L2 to 140 Hounsfield units, and L1 to 133 Hounsfield units (changes are consistent with osteopenia).

Included portions of the abdomen: Unremarkable.
doc_impression:
1. Multiple peribronchial subsolid pulmonary nodules with a tree-in-bud pattern. Multiple pulmonary bronchiectasis with decreased volume of the right middle lobe (middle lobe syndrome). Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, repeat computed tomography in 3–6 months is recommended. The above changes require correlation with history and clinical and laboratory data, to differentiate residual postinflammatory changes versus chronic inflammatory changes, including nontuberculous mycobacterial infection. Solitary solid nodules in the left lung. Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, repeat computed tomography in 3 months is recommended.

2. Mild coronary artery calcification. Consultation with the treating physician is recommended for comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or for semiquantitative scoring of coronary calcification for each of the four major coronary arteries.

3. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Computed tomography findings of osteopenia at T12–L2.

val_impression:
1. Multiple centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules and bronchocele in both lungs, сylindrical bronchiectasis in the right middle lobe, in the left lung, partial atelectasis of the right middle lobe. Findings are consistent with non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection (Lady Windermere syndrome) with associated middle lobe syndrome. The ACR Fleischner Society recommendations cannot be applied to the management of centrilobular nodules.

2. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Computed tomography findings of osteopenia at T12–L2.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:  
Previously identified part-solid and non-solid nodules are not visualized.  
In both lungs, multisegmental, a few micronodules up to 3 mm.  
In segment 10 of the right lung, paravertebral, an area of parenchymal ground-glass opacity persists without change in the setting of mild reticular opacity, likely fibrotic changes.  
Mild paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema, unchanged. Centrilobular bulla in segment 3 of the right lung up to 1.1 cm in diameter.  
No pleural effusion.  
Previously identified wall-adherent content in the trachea is not visualized.  
Mild conical narrowing of the right bronchus intermedius and right lower lobar bronchus persists, without change.  

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.  

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary arteries demonstrate a few calcified atherosclerotic plaques.  

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated up to 4.2 cm. The pulmonary trunk is not dilated. Calcification of the descending thoracic aorta.  

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the spine. Healed fractures of the anterior segments of the right ribs 2, 4, 5, 7, 9, and 10, and the posterior segments of the left ribs 4, 5, and 10.  

Included upper abdomen: Diffuse decreased liver attenuation persists to +35 Hounsfield units, with a round hypodense (+5 Hounsfield units) lesion up to 2.0 cm with smooth, well-defined margins. Multiple variably sized calcifications in the splenic parenchyma, unchanged.
doc_impression:
1. A few micronodules in both lungs. In high-risk patients, follow-up chest computed tomography is recommended in 12 months; in low-risk patients, follow-up imaging is not required.  

2. Dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta to 4.2 cm. Calcification of the thoracic aorta.  

3. Mixed emphysema.  

4. Focal pulmonary fibrosis in the right lower lobe.  

5. Mild coronary artery calcification. Consultation with the treating physician is recommended for comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or for semiquantitative coronary calcium scoring for each of the four major coronary arteries.  

6. Dilation of the pulmonary artery trunk. Clinical correlation is recommended for pulmonary arterial hypertension.  

7. Diffuse hepatic steatosis. Liver lesion, likely a cyst. Follow-up is not required.  

8. Degenerative changes of the spine. Multiple healed bilateral rib fractures.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No lymphadenopathy is identified. The fat of the upper mediastinum is diffusely increased in attenuation (due to residual thymic tissue?), otherwise no visible masses.

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification is identified.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with subchondral sclerosis of the vertebral endplates and osteophytes.  
In the T10 vertebral body, a sclerotic focus with irregular margins measures about 2.1 × 1.8 × 0.9 cm, attenuation about +1350 Hounsfield units, homogeneous structure.

Included upper abdomen: A hypodense fluid-attenuation lesion is subcapsular in liver segment 2, up to 1.0 × 0.7 cm, with sharp smooth margins. Along the liver capsule at the level of segment 4, single punctate calcifications.

Thyroid gland: Heterogeneous due to a hypodense nodule in the left lobe with attenuation about +40 Hounsfield units, measuring about 1.2 × 1.0 cm, with calcifications on both sides.  
Breasts: Partially included in the scan field, in the posterior third of the right breast at the junction of the upper quadrants, a mass is identified measuring about 1.5 × 1.5 cm, with sharp smooth margins.
doc_impression:
1. No pulmonary nodules requiring further follow-up per the 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations are identified.

2. Right breast mass. Correlate with dedicated breast imaging.

3. Sclerotic focus in the T10 vertebral body. Correlate with clinical history (in the absence of clinical manifestations and oncologic status: Bone Reporting and Data System category 1). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

4. Diffuse increased attenuation of the upper mediastinal fat may correspond to residual thymic tissue. Consider follow-up computed tomography in 6 to 12 months.

5. Thyroid nodules. Per “Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014”, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.

6. Hypodense lesion in the left liver lobe. Per “Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on Computed Tomography: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee 2017”, follow-up computed tomography is not required.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Areas of deformed intralobular and interlobular interstitium are present throughout all lung fields bilaterally, with areas of linear and focal fibrosis, with formation of multiple confluent air cysts with septations; changes are more pronounced in the posterior basal regions (honeycombing pattern).  
Aeration of the lung parenchyma is diffusely decreased throughout all lung fields; no evidence of acute infiltrative change is identified on this background. Subpleural calcified granuloma in the right lower lobe (segment 6, image 95) measuring up to 1.1 × 0.7 cm.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass is identified. Intrathoracic lymph nodes of all groups are enlarged, multiple; reference node measurements:  
- 4R group up to 1.4 cm in short axis; fatty hilum is present.  
- 4L group up to 1.4 cm in short axis; fatty hilum is present.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated (3.3 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased bone density of the vertebral bodies at T11–L2, with minimum attenuation up to 79 Hounsfield units (findings of osteoporosis).

Included portions of the abdomen: Abdominal aortic wall calcification.  
Included portions of the chest wall and neck: The thyroid gland is heterogeneous at the level of the study, with hypodense nodules up to 7 mm (right). In the left breast (partially included in the scan range), an asymmetric area of breast tissue is present (correlation with dedicated breast imaging is recommended). Axillary lymph nodes are not enlarged. Single left supraclavicular lymph node up to 8 mm in short axis.
doc_impression:
1. No pulmonary nodules requiring follow-up per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations. Findings, when correlated with history and clinical laboratory data, are most likely consistent with diffuse interstitial lung disease (idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis versus). Mediastinal lymphadenopathy (differentiate reactive etiology versus manifestations of sarcoidosis); pulmonology consultation is recommended.

2. Main pulmonary artery dilation up to 3.4 cm. This may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.

3. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Consultation with the treating physician is recommended for comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or semiquantitative coronary calcium scoring for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Asymmetric area of increased density in the left breast (compared with the contralateral side) requires correlation with dedicated breast imaging.

5. Aortic valve calcification.

6. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta.

7. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Computed tomography findings of osteoporosis (at the level of T11–L2 vertebral bodies).

8. Thyroid nodules (per “Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee,” 2014, no additional evaluation is required).

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Right lung is decreased in volume, with areas of linear and bandlike fibrosis; history of surgical treatment for lung cancer, and the lower lobe is compensatorily hyperexpanded. Along the suture material at the level of the stump of the right upper lobar bronchus, there is an area of consolidation of nonmeasurable size, irregular in shape with bandlike margins, involving the perihilar portions of the right lung. No significant bronchial narrowing is noted on this background, and the lumens are patent.  
In the left lung parenchyma, there are a few nodules, on series 5 presented as follows:  
In the left upper lobe:  
* Solid nodule up to 4 mm (image 134).  
* Solid nodule up to 3 mm (image 100).  
In the left lower lobe:  
* Subsolid nodule up to 7 mm (image 146).  
In the lung parenchyma, there are areas of paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. In the lower basal portions of the left lung, there are areas of mild ground-glass opacity with sharp margins.  
Pleural effusion: On the right, in the residual pleural space, there is a small amount of fluid (thickness about 1.2 cm); on the left, there is no effusion. Right pleura is thickened up to 8 mm; left pleura shows no visible change.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Mediastinum is mildly shifted to the right. Mediastinal lymph nodes are numerous, the largest short axis up to 1.4 cm (4R group). Right bronchopulmonary lymph nodes are difficult to differentiate on the background of the above-described area of consolidation.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is dilated in the ascending portion up to 4.0 cm, with wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.7 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Decreased vertebral body height at T6–T9 up to 32%, decreased vertebral density at T11–L1 (58–61 Hounsfield units). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.  
Areas of sclerotic bone remodeling in the anterior portions of the right fifth and sixth ribs, and in the posterior portion of the right eighth rib. Fractures of the posterior portions of the right sixth and seventh ribs with displacement of bone fragments and pseudoarthrosis formation.

Included abdomen: In the mid third of the right kidney parenchyma in the posterior segment, a hypodense lesion with sharp smooth margins and fluid attenuation, measuring about 2.0 × 1.5 cm.

Included neck: Right supraclavicular lymph nodes up to 1.0 cm in short axis.
doc_impression:
1. Status post surgical treatment for right lung cancer of unknown date. Computed tomography findings of an area of consolidation in the perihilar portions of the right lung; findings require correlation with clinical history and prior computed tomography, suspicious for recurrence; postoperative changes are less likely. Recommendation: Thoracic surgery consultation and bronchoscopy.  

2. Few solid and one subsolid lesions in the left lung. Fleischner Society recommendations cannot be applied given the clinical history. Follow-up should be performed per the oncologic surveillance protocol. Areas of emphysema. Small effusion in the residual right pleural space, and right costal pleural thickening (postoperative etiology?). Areas of mild ground-glass opacity in the left lower lobe (possibly postinflammatory).  

3. Mediastinal and right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.  

4. Dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, which may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.  

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification.  

6. Aortic valve calcification.  

7. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta.  

8. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased height of the T6–T9 vertebral bodies, computed tomography findings of osteoporosis. Areas of rib sclerosis of indeterminate nature (consider the question of performing bone scintigraphy), and right sixth and seventh rib fractures with pseudoarthrosis formation.  

9. Hypodense fluid-attenuation lesion of the right kidney: Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on Computed Tomography: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee 2018 is not applicable (given the history of oncologic disease).

Lungs and pleura: Resection of the upper right lobe, metal sutures are visualized along the mediastinum and the right upper lobar bronchus. On both sides of the metal sutures, a spiculated central mass is visualized, 5.6*3.2*6.0 cm, spreading into the middle and lower lobes, the lumen of the bronchial stump, with probable invasion of the mediastinum. Stump of the right upper lobar bronchus with thickening of the wall. Along the suture material in the right middle lobe there is an area of consolidation with smooth edges, 5.0*2.3 cm. 
Moderate paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema. In the lower lobes of both lungs there are multiple centrilobular areas of ground glass, mosaic pneumatization with air traps, the contents in the lumens of the subsegmental bronchi (small airways disease). In the lower lobe of the right lung there are linear opacities, may be an inflammatory process or hypoventilatory atelectasis. In the left lung subpleural reticulation and ground glass, consistent with interstitial changes.
In the left lung, there are a few nodules, on series 5 presented as follows:  
In the left upper lobe:  
* Solid nodule up to 4 mm (image 134).  
* Solid nodule up to 3 mm (image 100).  
In the left lower lobe:  
* Subsolid nodule up to 7 mm (image 146).  
On the right, there is a small amount of fluid in the pleural space (thickness about 1.2 cm); on the left, there is no effusion. Right pleura is thickened up to 8 mm.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Mediastinum is mildly shifted to the right. Mediastinal lymph nodes are numerous, the largest short axis up to 1.4 cm (4R group). Right bronchopulmonary lymph nodes are difficult to differentiate on the background of probable invasion of the mass.

Heart: No cardiomegaly, left ventricular enlargement. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic and mitral valve calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is dilated in the ascending portion up to 4.0 cm, with wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.7 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Multiple small osteoblastic lesions of the right 5th, 6th, 7th, 8th ribs. Fractures of the posterior segment of the right sixth rib and middle segment of the seventh rib with displacement of bone fragments. Decreased vertebral body height at T6–T9 up to 32%, decreased vertebral density at T11–L1 (58–61 HU). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Mild gynecomastia.

Included abdomen: In the mid third of the right kidney parenchyma in the posterior segment, a hypodense lesion with sharp smooth margins and fluid attenuation, measuring about 2.0 × 1.5 cm.

4. Multiple small osteoblastic lesions of the right 5th, 6th, 7th, 8th ribs. Fractures of the posterior segment of the right sixth rib and middle segment of the seventh rib with displacement of bone fragments, may be pathological fractures.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased height of the T6–T9 vertebral bodies, computed tomography findings of osteoporosis. 

8. Hypodense fluid-attenuation lesion of the right kidney: Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on Computed Tomography: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee 2018 is not applicable (given the history of oncologic disease).

Heterogeneous pleural thickening on the right (image 167-216), measures 3.0 × 5.4 × 1.2 cm, contains fat and solid components, with irregular margins. val_impression:
1. Multiloculated cyst in the right upper lobe measures 1.2 × 1.2 × 1.0 cm. According to ACR Lung-RADS v2022 multilocular cysts are classified as Lung-RADS 4A with recommended management of 3-month CT or PET/CT if there is a solid component of at least 8 mm.

2. Multiple solid and subsolid nodules in the upper lobes. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Follow-up chest CT at 3-6 months to confirm persistence. If stable, consider chest CT at 2 and 4 years.

3. Heterogeneous pleural thickening on the right measures 3.0 × 5.4 × 1.2 cm, contains fat and solid components, may correspond to neoplasm. Compared to the Abdomen CT on 2025-09-12, there is marked interval worsening due to the development of a solid component.

4. Mild paraseptal emphysema in the apices. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

5. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

7. Left adrenal lesion measuring < 1 cm. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following:  No need for further imaging follow-up.

8. Hepatic cysts. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: Benign lesion. No need for further follow-up.

doc_impression:

CT follow-up in 3 months is appropriate. Pulmonology and thoracic surgery consultation is appropriate to determine whether biopsy should be performed.

val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Centrilobular pulmonary emphysema, without change. Stable linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe and in the lingular and basal segments on the left, new linear atelectases in the left upper lobe (segments 2 and 3). 

In the right lower lobe, there is persistent ground-glass opacity (series 4, image 193) measuring 2.6 × 2.4 cm (previously 1.5 × 1.5 cm).  

Also, there are persistent multiple pulmonary nodules (series 4) and perifissural nodes:  
In the right upper lobe:  
* Solid nodule measuring up to 6 mm (image 126), without change.  
* Peribronchial nodules of varying density forming a “tree-in-bud” pattern, up to 3–4 mm (image 101), previously less clearly visualized.  
* Multiple ground-glass and solid nodules in the subpleural regions (images 65–75) up to 4 mm, without change.  

In the right middle lobe:  
* Perifissural ground-glass nodule measuring up to 9 mm (image 157), previously less clearly visualized, up to 8 mm.  

In the left lower lobe:  
* Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 106), without change.  

No pleural effusion. Along the wall of the trachea and large bronchi, there is mucous bronchial contents.  

Mediastinum and lung hila: The mediastinum is slightly shifted to the left. No mass and no lymphadenopathy are identified.  

Heart: Mild cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate aortic valve calcification. Moderate coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.7 cm (previously 3.8 cm).  

Osseous structures and chest wall: Decreased density of bone tissue in the vertebral bodies T11-L1 to 70 HU (consistent with osteoporosis). Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.  

Included abdomen: Calcification of the walls of the abdominal aorta, celiac trunk, and splenic artery.
val_impression:
1. The ground-glass opacity has increased in size to 2.6 x 2.4 cm (compared to 1.5 x 1.5 cm on the prior study). Given the interval growth, findings are highly concerning for a slow-growing malignancy.

2. Compared to the prior study, there are new right upper lobe nodules forming a "tree-in-bud" pattern, suggestive of bronchiolitis. The remaining pulmonary nodules are stable or demonstrate a minimal increase in size. Pulmonary emphysema. Bilateral atelectasis.

3. Mild cardiomegaly. Main pulmonary artery dilatation (3.7 cm), which may be consistent with pulmonary hypertension. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  Moderate aortic valve calcification. Moderate atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta, abdominal aorta, and branches.  

4. Findings consistent with osteoporosis at T11-L1 and moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation.

val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In the right middle lobe and in both lobes of the left lung multiple calcifications measure up to 4 mm. Multiple small ground-glass opacities with a tree-in-bud pattern are noted in the right middle lobe with a 5 mm ground-glass nodule is seen within the same region (series 5, image 130). In the lung parenchyma multiple nodules are present.  

In the left upper lobe (series 5):  
* 4 mm ground-glass nodule (image 27).  
* 5 mm solid nodule (image 61).  
In the right lower lobe (series 5):  
* 3 mm solid nodule (image 113).  

In the right lung, single micronodules are present. Linear atelectasis in the lower portions of both lungs in the setting of a hiatal hernia.  No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy. The gastric cardia and partially the gastric body herniate into the posterior mediastinum, forming a hiatal hernia measuring approximately 7.9 × 5.6 cm; diaphragmatic crus separation is 2.6 cm.  

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Minimal calcification of the aortic valve and moderate calcification of the mitral valve. Moderate coronary artery calcification. 

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.  

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 117 HU, T12 - 110 HU (findings consistent with osteopenia), and L1 - 97 HU, L2 - 90 HU (findings consistent with osteoporosis).  

Included upper abdomen: Calcified colonic diverticula without acute diverticulitis. Calcified abdominal aortic wall. In the left paracolic gutter, there is a nodular structure up to 9 mm with density similar to the splenic parenchyma (likely an accessory spleen). In the right liver lobe a single hypodense lesion up to 7 × 6 mm with indistinct margins (series 3, image 185).
val_impression:
1.  Bronchovascular ground-glass tree-in-bud pattern in the right middle lobe, findings are consistent with a post infectious/inflammatory process. Multiple ground-glass and solid nodules in both lungs measure up to 5 mm. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

2. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Minimal calcification of the aortic valve and moderate calcification of the mitral valve. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta.

5. Small hypodense lesion in the right liver lobe with indistinct margins. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, follow-up MRI in 3 to 6 months is recommended for further evaluation.

6. Hiatal hernia without evidence of strangulation, causing minimal compressive changes of the adjacent lung parenchyma.

7. Calcified colonic diverticula without acute diverticulitis.

8. Round nodular structure in the left paracolic gutter, likely an accessory spleen.

9. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophyte formation. Findings consistent with osteopenia at T11-T12 and osteoporosis at L1-L2.

doc_impression:
1. Small pulmonary nodules. Considering absence of risk factors, per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no further follow-up is required.

2. Moderate coronary artery calcification.

3. Minimal calcification of the aortic valve and calcification of the mitral valve.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta.

5. Small hypodense lesion in the liver with indistinct margins. Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, magnetic resonance imaging is recommended in the near term.

6. Hiatal hernia with no findings of strangulation, with minimal compressive changes of the adjacent lung parenchyma.

7. Calcified colonic diverticula with no findings of diverticulitis.

8. Round nodular structure in the left paracolic gutter (near the inferior pole of the spleen). This may correspond to an accessory spleen; confident assessment is limited on a noncontrast examination.

9. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. CT findings of osteopenia at T11-T12 and osteoporosis at L1-L2.

doc_impression:
1.

Diffuse lung changes: Peribronchial nodules in a tree-in-bud pattern, areas of consolidation in the left lingular segments, interlobular interstitial thickening, and diffuse ground-glass opacity, likely inflammatory (bronchiolitis versus bronchopneumonia). Improvement compared with January 13, 2026, with decreased areas of consolidation and decreased intensity of changes. Recommend CT follow-up of the chest in 6–8 weeks.

2.

Multiple areas of paraseptal and centrilobular emphysema.

3.

**INDICATIONS:**  
Diagnosis: Malignant neoplasm of the anus, solitary pulmonary nodule.  
History: Surveillance for pulmonary nodules, asymptomatic, non-smoker, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, history of colorectal cancer diagnosis in 2023.  
Trauma: No.  
Surgeries: Not in this region.  
Oncology: Colorectal cancer, diagnosed in 2019, chemotherapy, radiation therapy.

**TECHNIQUE:** Scans were obtained through the thorax without intravenous contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).  

**RADIATION DOSE:** CTDI volume [VALUE] mGy; total DLP [VALUE] mGy-cm.  

Previously performed CT or radionuclide cardiac studies within the last 12 months: No data available.  

## FINDINGS:
**Lungs and pleura:**  
Peribronchial tree-in-bud nodules in the right lower lobe and mild in the left lower lobe. Single areas of consolidation in the left lower lobe, with a dilated subsegmental bronchus B9 (no change). A single nodule in the left upper lobe measuring 6 mm on image 26 (no change). Small areas of apical fibrosis bilaterally. No pleural effusion.  

**Mediastinum and hila:** No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.  

**Heart:** No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.  

**Great vessels:** The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.  

**Osseous structures and chest wall:** Decreased density of the osseous structures at the imaged level to +37 HU at the L1 level. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.  

**Included portions of the abdomen:** Hiatal hernia measuring up to 3.2 × 2.5 cm. In the liver, single hypodense lesions up to 7 × 5 mm (likely cysts). The left adrenal gland is diffusely thickened up to 8 mm. In the pancreatic head, a cystic structure is visualized measuring up to 2.8 × 1.9 cm.  

##
doc_impression:
1. Peribronchial tree-in-bud changes in the lower lobes of both lungs and a single consolidation in the left lower lobe with dilation of the subsegmental bronchus, likely inflammatory changes (bronchiolitis versus bronchopneumonia). CT follow-up of the chest is recommended in 6–8 weeks.  

2. Solid 6 mm nodule in the left upper lobe, unchanged compared with August 18, 2025. More likely post-inflammatory in etiology; however, given the oncologic history, CT follow-up is recommended in 3–6 months (per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations for high-risk patients).  

3. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.  

4. Decreased bone density (to 37 HU at the L1 level), findings of osteoporosis.  

5. Hiatal hernia.  

6. Small hypodense liver lesions, likely cysts, do not require follow-up.  

7. Cystic lesion in the pancreatic head, may correspond to a dilated common bile duct. Further evaluation with magnetic resonance imaging or multiphasic CT is recommended for clarification.  

8. Diffuse thickening of the left adrenal gland without evidence of a mass. No follow-up is required.

2. Peribronchial solid 4 mm nodule in the right upper lobe and subsolid 3 mm nodule in the right middle lobe. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of lung cancer, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of lung cancer, generally no need for follow-up. An optional follow-up chest CT at 12 months could be performed if there is high index of suspicion. If unchanged, no further follow-up is necessary.

3. Moderate dilation of the main pulmonary artery to 3.0 cm, which may indicate pulmonary hypertension.

4. Moderate coronary artery calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. 

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine at T2–L1.

6. Decreased liver density (to 35 HU), consistent with moderate hepatic steatosis. A few well-defined fat-density lesions in the spleen measuring up to 1.0 cm with benign appearance, do not require follow-up. Cholecystectomy. 

7. Hypodense thyroid nodules measuring up to 8 mm. ACR White Paper recommendations (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.

val_findings:

On the right in all lobes, small centrilobular confluent nodules of ground glass; previously, the centrilobular nodules were denser, without the formation of large areas of ground glass. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy. There is uneven dilatation of the esophageal lumen with a large amount of fluid content. There is a hiatal hernia with herniation of a portion of the stomach into the inferior posterior mediastinum, measuring 4.9 × 3.8 cm.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated to 4.2 cm at the level of the ascending portion, with moderate wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.5 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes and scoliosis of the thoracic spine.

Included upper abdomen: In the left retroperitoneum, there is a hypodense lesion measuring up to 1.7 cm, with smooth, well-defined margins, not fully included in the scan range (per CT from February 11, 2026, the lesion arises from the left kidney), benign, no follow-up required.

Thyroid gland: in the right lobe of the thyroid gland there is a heterogeneous lesion with calcification, with substernal extension, measuring up to 2.1 × 2.5 cm. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere. Hypodense lesion of the left lobe of the thyroid gland, 7 mm (no need for further follow-up).

val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Clustered centrilobular nodules persist in the left lower lobe, forming a "tree-in-bud" pattern, compared with the prior study with decreased size:
- The largest nodule previously up to 1.3 × 0.8 cm (series 5, image 112) has decreased to 6 mm on the current study, is less rounded, and has more linear margins.
- The largest nodule on the current study is up to 8 mm (series 5, image 104), previously up to 9 mm.
The remaining previously noted solid and calcified nodules (single) show no interval change, shown on series 5:
In the right lung:
- In the upper lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm, partially calcified (image 68).
- In the middle lobe, a subpleural nodule up to 3 mm (image 103).
Single calcifications in the left upper lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change).
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: Partly calcified aortopulmonary window lymph node measures 1.5 cm in short axis, no interval change. Nonenlarged lower paratracheal lymph node with single calcifications, no interval change.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with mild wall calcifications. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with formation of small osteophytes. Decrease in vertebral body density: T11 - 148 HU, T12 - 129 HU, L1 - 101 HU, L2 - 100 HU.

Included portions of the abdomen: Calcification in the right hepatic lobe up to 5 mm (no interval change). No need for further follow-up.

Thyroid: heterogeneous structure due to nodules; the largest on the left is up to 2.9 × 2.7 cm (no interval change), with mild extension into the superior mediastinum. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.
val_impression:
1. Decreased in size, clustered centrilobular nodules in the left lower lobe, consistent with sequelae of known coccidiomycosis. No new pulmonary nodules. Stable solid and partially calcified pulmonary nodules. Per Fleischner Society recommendations 2017, no further follow-up is required.

2. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta. No coronary artery calcification.

3. Stable partly calcified mediastinal lymph nodes, sequelae of granulomatous disease.

4. Stable thyroid nodules. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia.

In the left lung, a solitary micronodule up to 2 mm is present in the upper lobe (slice 49).  
No pleural effusion.  
The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesion or lymphadenopathy.

doc_impression:
1. Solitary pulmonary nodules. Per Fleischner Society 2017 recommendations, no additional CT follow-up is required.  

2. Mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Small pericardial effusion.  

3. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.  

4. Hiatal hernia.  

5. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and cervical osteophytes within the imaged field. Bone lucency focus in the xiphoid process is too small for assessment. Bone-RADS 3, CT follow-up in 6 months.  

6. Nodular structure at the inferior margin of the left thyroid lobe on this noncontrast study, of unclear etiology (accessory thyroid tissue versus supraclavicular lymph node). Correlate with ultrasound.  

7. Thickening of the left adrenal gland. Per Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee, 2017, CT follow-up is not required.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:  
In both lungs: Pleuroapical fibrosis; areas of mild centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema; plate-like atelectasis in the anterior portions on the right and in lung bases on the left.  
In the left lung: Reticular changes in the posterior portions.  
In the right lung: Calcified nodule in the lower lobe measuring 5 mm on image 178.  
No pleural effusion.

Pulmonary solid nodules in series 6 are as follows:  
Right middle lobe:  
Nodule measuring 6 mm on image 160.

Mediastinum and hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Augmentation mammoplasty.

Included portions of the abdomen: Cholecystectomy. Three hypodense lesions in the liver in segments 4, 5, and 6, round, homogeneous, with indistinct margins, with maximum size 1.3 × 1.5 cm (segment 4). Two hypodense lesions in the body of the left adrenal gland, round, measuring 1.1 × 1.0 × 1.3 cm, density 4 HU, and up to 7 mm in diameter, density -46 HU. Along the posterior contour of the left kidney, a round hypodense lesion measuring up to 8 mm in diameter, density -23 HU. The left renal collecting system is dilated: renal pelvis up to 1.7 cm; no evidence of calyceal dilatation.
doc_impression:
1. Solid nodule up to 6 mm in the right middle lobe. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, CT follow-up in 12 months. Calcified pulmonary nodule in the right lower lobe, not requiring follow-up.

2. Mild pulmonary emphysema.

3. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe.

4. Cholecystectomy.

5. Hypodense lesion in the left kidney (likely a benign cyst; no further follow-up is required).

6. Hypodense liver lesions (likely benign cysts; no further follow-up is required).

7. Left pyelectasis (renal ultrasound is recommended).

8. Hypodense lesions in the right adrenal gland (CT follow-up in 12 months is recommended).

In the right middle lobe, solid nodule measures 6 mm on image 160.
Multiple micronodules in both lungs, mostly subpleural and centrilobular, up to 1–2 mm (image 82, 87, 95, 99, 113, 154, 164).
No pleural effusion.

val_impression:
1. Solid nodule measures 6 mm in the right middle lobe. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: Initial follow-up chest CT at 6-12 months. If stable, another follow-up chest CT at 18-24 months. Calcified pulmonary nodule in the right lower lobe, not requiring follow-up. Multiple micronodules in both lungs, mostly subpleural and centrilobular, may be inflammatory process/respiratory bronchiolitis.

2. Mild centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Cholecystectomy. Cysts in the left kidney, likely benign; no further follow-up is required. Hypodense liver lesions, likely benign cysts; no further follow-up is required.

5. Hypodense lesions in the right adrenal gland. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: Benign adenoma. No need for further imaging follow-up. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status.

doc_findings:
The examination is affected by artifacts. Visualization is decreased:

Lungs and pleura: Centrilobular emphysema in the lungs (more pronounced in the upper lobes). Mild areas of pleuroapical fibrosis.

Right lung:
Previously noted solid nodule in the apical regions with increased size to 2.3 × 1.7 cm, previously up to 1.8 × 1.6 cm by independent repeat measurement. The margins of the above-described nodule are spiculated, including a thickened band extending to the costal pleura.
In the lower lobe, two adjacent solid nodules measure up to 2 mm (series 5 image 181) and were not clearly differentiated previously.
Calcified granuloma in the lower lobe measuring up to 4 mm.

Left lung:
In the left upper lobe, a calcified lesion with thin peripheral linear bands is present (no change), series 5 image 111.

Right pleural effusion with layer thickness up to 1.8 cm (previously absent). No left pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy. Scattered intrathoracic lymph nodes with calcifications.

Heart: Cardiac chambers are mildly enlarged. Distal portions of pacemaker leads (generator most recently implanted in the soft tissues of the left pectoral region) are in the right cardiac chambers. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary calcification. Aortic valve calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated (4.2 cm) with wall calcification. The pulmonary trunk is dilated (3.3 cm).

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased vertebral body density at the T11-L2 levels up to +126 HU.

Included abdomen: Visualization is decreased due to motion artifacts. Areas of pancreatic lipomatosis.
doc_impression:
1.

Right upper lobe nodule has increased in size. Findings are suspicious for a neoplastic etiology. Morphologic verification is recommended. Consider positron emission tomography-computed tomography. New solid nodules in the right lower lobe are present; given the above, a secondary etiology cannot be excluded. Single calcified lesion in the left upper lobe shows no change and may correspond to a healed granuloma; no follow-up is required. Pulmonary emphysema. Small right pleural effusion (previously not present).

2.

Pulmonary trunk dilation up to 3.4 cm. This may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.

3.

Moderate coronary calcification. Pacemaker placement.

4.

Aortic valve calcification.

5.

Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta with aneurysmal dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta.

6.

Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. CT findings of osteopenia at the T11-L2 levels.

Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema. Linear atelectasis in both lower lobes. No pleural effusion. Significant thickening of the walls of the subsegmental bronchi, including the upper lobes, which corresponds to bronchitis.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass. No lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

Great vessels: Dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta measures up to 4.0 cm, with severe wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Diffuse heterogeneous decreased density of vertebral bodies (maximum down to 102 HU). Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints, more pronounced on the left.

Included upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression:
1. Decreased in size, part-solid nodule, measuring 6 mm in the right lower lobe, may have an inflammatory etiology. According to Fleischner Society Recommendation, part-solid nodules may be due to transient infections and may resolve after short-term follow-up. Unchanged solid nodules in the left upper lobe require no follow up. Multiple centrilobular micronodules and ground-glass nodules may represent smoking-related disease (respiratory bronchiolitis). Significant thickening of the walls of the subsegmental bronchi, including the upper lobes, which corresponds to bronchitis.

2. Moderate centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema in both lungs. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening.

3. Calcified granulomas in both lungs, consistent with prior infection. No follow-up is required.

4. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

5. Dilation of the ascending thoracic aorta, up to 4.0 cm.

6. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Degenerative changes of the shoulder joints.

doc_impression:
1. Multiple pulmonary micronodules in both lungs and solid nodules in both lower lobes. Per Fleischner 2017 recommendations, consider CT in 3 months, positron emission tomography and CT, or biopsy of the nodule. Peribronchovascular micronodules in the right middle lobe and left lower lobe may correspond to bronchiolitis.

2. Confluent centrilobular emphysema. Platelike atelectasis in both lungs. Thick-walled cyst in the right lower lobe.

3. Cardiomegaly. Mild pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Left kidney lesions. Per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, CT or magnetic resonance imaging without and with contrast is recommended (magnetic resonance imaging is preferred).

5. Hemorrhagic or proteinaceous cyst of the left kidney. Per Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2018, no further evaluation is required. Nonobstructing right renal calculus.

6. Thickening of the left adrenal gland.

7. Cystic lesion of the pancreatic tail. Per Management of Incidental Pancreatic Cysts: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2017, follow-up study once every 2 years.

8. Diverticula of the colonic wall.

9. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia/osteoporosis.

10. Thyroid lesion. Per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee, 2014, no further follow-up is required.

11. Cholecystectomy.

val_impression:
1. Suspicious nodule measures 2.5 × 1.6 cm in the right lower lobe and may be of neoplastic etiology. Consider PET CT or biopsy. 
Multiple solid nodules in both lungs. Centrilobular nodules in the right middle lobe and left lower lobe may correspond to bronchiolitis.

2. Severe confluent centrilobular emphysema. Subsegmental atelectasis in the lingual segment against the background of bronchial secretion. 

3. Coronary calcification, qualitatively assessed as severe. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Cardiomegaly. Calcifications of the aortic and mitral valves.

4. Mild pericardial effusion. 

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Diffuse decreased density of vertebral bodies consistent with osteoporosis.

6. Two indeterminate lesions of the left kidney. ACR White Paper recommendations (Herts, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: Further evaluation with CT or MR without and with IV contrast recommended. Hemorrhagic or proteinaceous cyst of the left kidney. Per ACR White Paper recommendations, no further evaluation is required. Non Nonobstructing right renal calculus.

7. Right adrenal lesion measures 10 mm, 18 HU. ACR White Paper recommendations (Mayo-Smith, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1038-1044) suggest the following: For patients without cancer history, follow-up study with adrenal protocol CT in 12 months recommended. Consider biochemical evaluation to determine functional status and exclude pheochromocytoma. For patients with positive cancer history, prompt further evaluation with adrenal protocol CT recommended.
Left adrenal lesion measures 11 mm, 7 HU, benign lesion. No need for further imaging follow-up.

8. Cystic lesion of the pancreatic tail. Per Management of Incidental Pancreatic ACR White Paper recommendations (Megibow, et al. J AM Coll Radiol 2017; 14: 911-923) suggest the following: Follow-up pancreas-protocol CT or MRI with contrast at 2 years and 4 years after the initial discovery of the cyst or after a growing cyst becomes stable again.

9. Thyroid lesion. Per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee, 2014, no further follow-up is required.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:  
The right hemidiaphragm is elevated, the basilar segments of the right lung are compressed, and aeration of the right lower lobe is decreased (visually without significant change). Less pronounced linear atelectasis is present in the right middle lobe and in the basal segments on the left (visually unchanged).  
In the right lower lobe (series 5, image 127), a subpleural focal area of consolidation persists, measuring about 2.5 × 1.3 cm (previously about 2.4 × 1.3 cm), with band-like margins, with small air lucencies up to 4 mm (previously up to 3 mm).  

In the aerated portions of the left lung, solitary solid nodules persist in the same number (series 5):  
In the upper lobe:  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 6 mm (image 59), with calcification, unchanged.  
In the lower lobe:  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 5 mm (image 161), with calcification, unchanged.  
* Subpleural nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 187), with calcification, previously up to 3 mm.  

Solitary calcifications are present in the lungs, the largest in the right upper lobe measuring up to 5 mm.  
The right costal pleura at the level of the posterior segment of the 3rd rib/intercostal space shows focal thickening up to 9 mm (unchanged).  
No pleural effusion.  

Mild atelectasis is noted in the right lower lobe and an infiltrate in a ground-glass pattern. Focal pleural-based parenchymal thickening in the mediobasal part of the right lower lobe measures 1.7 cm. Additionally, hypodense renal nodules are noted in the costophrenic angle. In the lateral part of the mid zone of the left lung, a pleural-based pleural nodule measuring 6 mm is also identified and is stable.  

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.  

Heart: The cardiac chambers are mildly enlarged. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Mild coronary calcification.  

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is dilated in the ascending portion up to 4.6 cm (unchanged), the descending aorta is not dilated, and the walls are calcified. The pulmonary trunk is dilated up to 3.4 cm (unchanged by the author’s measurements).  

Osseous structures and chest wall: Decreased height of the T12 vertebral body up to 32%, decreased density of vertebral bodies T11 and L1 to +84 HU (CT signs of osteoporosis). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes.  

Included portions of the abdomen are unchanged: Numerous hypodense lesions in the liver with fluid density, the largest at the border of segments 5 and 6 measuring up to 4.1 × 3.3 cm; closer to the paracaval portions of segment 5, a solitary lesion with a peripheral calcification up to 3 mm (series 4, image 211).  
In the lateral limb of the left adrenal gland, a low-density nodular structure (-5 HU) measures about 9 × 9 mm (series 4, image 235).
doc_impression:
1. Subpleural focal consolidation in the right lower lobe may correspond to rounded atelectasis; against this background, the presence or absence of malignant changes cannot be excluded. Continue CT follow-up in 6–12 months. No significant change compared with the prior study. Focal thickening of the right costal pleura at the level of the right upper lobe is unchanged (postinflammatory changes versus; correlate with clinical history).  

2. Solitary solid partially calcified nodules in the left lung are unchanged (likely postinflammatory). If risk factors are present, follow-up chest CT may be performed in 12 months; if no such risk factors are present, per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no further follow-up is required.  

3. Dilatation of the pulmonary trunk up to 3.4 cm. This may be a sign of pulmonary hypertension.  

4. Cardiomegaly and mild coronary calcification. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  

5. Aortic valve calcification.  

6. Aneurysmal dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta (unchanged) and atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.  

7. CT signs of osteoporosis (T11–L1). Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.  

8. Multiple hypodense liver lesions with fluid structure (may correspond to cysts; in a noncontrast study, complete evaluation is not possible), unchanged. Per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, if no risk factors are present, follow-up is not required.  

9. Small left adrenal nodule with a benign phenotype. Per Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, follow-up is not required.

Lungs and pleura: Bilateral diffuse subpleural areas of ground glass opacities, reticulations, and traction bronchiectasis are stable. Bilateral apical scarring is present with traction bronchiectasis.
In the left upper lobe, a peribronchial area of consolidation with ground glass around the periphery, series 5, images 82-92, previously not visualized.
In the left upper lobe, a subpleural area of consolidation, probably with pseudocavitation, is present, series 5, images 108-123 (no interval change).
Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:
Right middle lobe:
- Solid nodule measures 3 mm on image 86 (previously not visualized).
Right lower lobe:
- Solid nodule measures 4 mm on image 120 with cavitation (previously not visualized).
- Calcified granuloma measures 2 mm on image 128 (no interval change).
Left upper lobe:
- Calcified granuloma measures 5 mm on image 42 (no interval change).
- Solid nodules measure 4 mm on images 90-95 (previously not visualized).
Left lower lobe:
- Solid nodules measure up to 6 mm on images 96-99 (previously not visualized).
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy are identified. Calcifications are present in a few nonenlarged intrathoracic lymph nodes.

Heart: Enlargement of the left chambers of the heart is present, with calcifications in the left ventricle. No clinically significant pericardial effusion is present. Coronary calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as severe. Mitral valve prosthesis. Reduced attenuation of the intracardiac blood within the left ventricle, associated with anemia.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with moderate wall calcification. The pulmonary trunk is dilated to 3.0 cm.

Osseous structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and shoulder joints. Decrease in vertebral body density: T11 - 85 HU, T12 - 80 HU.

val_impression:
1. New area of consolidation in the left upper lobe, new multiple nodules in both lungs, stable area of consolidation, probably with pseudocavitation. Negative interval change compared with December 22, 2025, findings possibly consistent with chronic coccidioidomycosis.

2. Interstitial lung disease may correspond to nonspecific interstitial pneumonia or possible usual interstitial pneumonia.

3. Calcified pulmonary nodules and calcification of mediastinal lymph nodes, consistent with prior infection.

4. Severe coronary artery calcification. Enlargement of the left chambers of the heart, calcifications in the left ventricle. Mitral valve prosthesis. Reduced attenuation of the intracardiac blood within the left ventricle, associated with anemia.

5. Dilated main pulmonary artery (30 mm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

6. Left renal non nonobstructing calculus. 

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and shoulder joints. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteoporosis.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Hypostatic changes in the posterior basal lung bases. Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingular segments, and basal segments on the left.  
Mild ground-glass opacities in the right lower lobe (visually unchanged), likely hypoventilatory in nature (the prior study was performed on incomplete inspiration, and the changes were more pronounced).  
Single solid nodules are identified in the lung parenchyma, in series 5 as follows:  
In the right lung:  
In the middle lobe, a nodule measures up to 3 mm (image 103), with calcification, not previously visualized.  
In the left lung:  
Along the interlobar fissure, up to 6 mm (image 113), unchanged.

No pleural effusion. Costal pleura in the right supradiaphragmatic regions with areas of calcification, visually unchanged.  
The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy are identified.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Distal portions of the cardiac pacemaker (implanted in the left pectoral region) are within the lumen of the right heart chambers. Aortic valve calcification. Pericardial fluid measures up to 1.2 cm in thickness. Moderate coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated, with wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.2 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased density of bone tissue in vertebrae at the T11 level is less than 100 HU (CT signs of osteoporosis). Decreased height and wedge deformity of the T8 vertebral body up to 26%.  
Healing fractures of the posterior segments of the right 5th, 6th, and 7th ribs (at the level of the necks).

Upper abdomen:  
In the liver parenchyma (segment 8), a hypodense lesion with well-defined smooth margins and fluid density measures up to 7 × 6 mm; a similar lesion in segment 2 measures up to 9 × 9 mm (previous visualization was unsatisfactory). The gallbladder is not visualized (removed?), with dilation of the lobar bile ducts up to 1.1 cm, and of the common bile duct up to 1.2 cm; the density of the parenchyma in the subpleural portions of segment 4 is decreased, visually unchanged at the level of the study.

Thyroid gland: Heterogeneous structure due to indistinct hypodense lesions up to 1.2 × 1.2 cm.
doc_impression:
1. Solid 6 mm nodule along the left interlobar pleura, unchanged compared with the prior study. If risk factors are present, follow-up CT of the chest may be performed in 12 months. If no such risk factors, per Society recommendations.

2. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.2 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

3. Moderate coronary calcification. Cardiac pacemaker placement.

4. Aortic valve calcification.

5. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

6. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophyte formation. Decreased height of the T8 vertebral body. Decreased vertebral body density consistent with osteopenia/osteoporosis.

7. Hypodense liver lesions, areas of decreased liver parenchymal density, and bile duct dilation: per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee 2017, MRI is recommended in the near term.

8. Thyroid nodules: per Managing Incidental Thyroid Nodules Detected on Imaging: White Paper of the ACR Incidental Thyroid Findings Committee 2014, thyroid ultrasound is recommended.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

4. Pleuroapical fibrosis, discoid atelectasis, and traction bronchiectasis in both lungs.

5. Mild pulmonary emphysema.

10. Bilateral Bochdalek hernias.

val_findings:

Bilateral diffuse mosaic attenuation patterns representing air trapping, more pronounced in the lower lobes, middle lobe and lingual segments, due to bronchial obstruction. The walls of the bronchi of the lower lobes, middle lobe and lingual segments are severely thickened, subsegmental bronchi are obstructed by contents, in some bronchi by calcified contents. In the upper lobes and to a lesser extent in the lower lobes, multiple centrilobular nodules and centrilobular ground-glass opacities. In the upper lobes, thickening of the interlobular interstitium. Micronodules in both lungs measure up to 2–3 mm in diameter. Discoid atelectasis against the background of bilateral Bochdalek hernias.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: The heart is enlarged, cardiothoracic ratio is 0.67. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary calcification, qualitatively moderate. Calcification of the mitral and aortic valves.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques are present in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. 
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-T12 level to 122 HU (CT signs of demineralization). L1 retrolisthesis, 4 mm, grade I according to Meyerding classification.

Upper abdomen: Diverticulosis of the transverse colon without signs of inflammation. In the upper pole of the left kidney, a calculus measures up to 2 mm in diameter.
val_impression:
1. Diffuse bronchial disease with bronchial contents may represent obliterating bronchiolitis or diffuse panbronchiolitis. Distal airways disease in the form of centrilobular opacities. 

2. Cardiomegaly. Calcification of the mitral and aortic valves.

3. Coronary calcification, qualitatively moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Diverticulosis of the transverse colon.

5. Nonobstructing calculus of the left kidney.

6. L1 retrolisthesis, 4 mm, grade I according to Meyerding classification.

val_impression:

2. Mild paraseptal emphysema. Poorly defined centrilobular ground-glass opacities and mucous contents of small bronchi probably correspond to smoking related respiratory bronchiolitis.

3. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending and right coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Single liver lesion up to 6 mm. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following:
- For patients with low risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, no need for further follow-up.
- For patients with high risk of having a malignant hepatic lesion, follow-up MRI in 3 to 6 months is recommended for further evaluation.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
In both lungs, pleuroapical fibrosis; areas of mild centrilobular emphysema; in the lower lobes of both lungs, micronodules measuring up to 3 mm in diameter; regression of some previously identified nodules in the lower lobes of both lungs.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and lung hila: No mass lesions or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

Upper abdomen: In the liver, round hypodense lesions with homogeneous structure and smooth well-defined margins, measuring up to 8 × 6 mm (segment 8), with no interval change compared with March 17, 2025. A round heterogeneous lesion in the upper segment of the left kidney due to a small calcification, measuring 1.1 × 0.7 × 0.9 cm, density 44 HU, with negative interval change compared with March 17, 2025.
doc_impression:
1. Heterogeneous lesion in the left kidney with negative interval change compared with March 17, 2025, indeterminate. Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI is recommended.

2. Micronodules in the lower lobes of both lungs. Compared with March 17, 2025, positive interval change with regression of some nodules. No nodules are identified that require follow-up per Fleischner Society recommendations.

3. Mild pulmonary emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; the current study may be used as a baseline for subsequent follow-up.

4. Hypodense liver lesions, stable compared with March 17, 2025 (likely benign cysts not requiring follow-up).

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
The study is performed without full inspiration and adequate breath-holding.

Lungs and pleura:
Mosaic attenuation of the lungs. Artifacts from suboptimal breathing for scanning. Against this background: In the left upper lobe, a ground-glass opacity area is present, likely due to suboptimal breathing, and linear atelectasis; no nodules requiring follow-up are identified; a few calcified micronodules measure up to 3 mm.
In the left lower lobe, there is an extensive area of consolidation with preserved bronchial lumina within it (series 5, images 102-155).
A few areas of centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema.
On the left, minimal pleural effusion, including along the interlobar pleura.
Dependent changes in lung bases.
The ground-glass opacity areas seen on the 2019 study are not identified.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses are identified. A single enlarged lower paratracheal lymph node measures up to 1.2 cm in short axis (series 4, image 72), with no change.

Heart: Cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Moderate coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.0 cm. Multiple calcifications of the aortic arch and aortic valve.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Osteoarthritis of the shoulder joints (no change).

Upper abdomen: Cholecystectomy.
doc_impression:
1. Extensive consolidation in the left lower lobe, inflammatory in nature.

2. Minimal left pleural effusion.

3. Mild emphysema, with no change.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.0 cm) and cardiomegaly, which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

5. A single enlarged lower paratracheal lymph node measuring up to 1.2 cm, with no change.

6. Moderate coronary calcification and aortic calcification, and aortic valve calcification, with no change.

7. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and osteoarthritis of the shoulder joints, with no change.

8. The ground-glass opacity area(s) seen on the 2019 study are not identified.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: The membranous portion of the trachea prolapses into the lumen (incomplete inspiration). Pronounced respiratory artifacts and pronounced gravitational changes are present. In the left upper lobe, a large ground-glass area is present. In the left lower lobe, an extensive area of consolidation with preserved bronchial lumina is present (series 5, images 102-155). In the right lung, in the lower and middle lobes, subsegmental atelectasis is present. Previously seen cysts in both lungs remain. In the left upper lobe, a calcified nodule measures 5 mm, unchanged. In the left lung, a few calcified nodules measure up to 3 mm. No nodules requiring follow-up are identified. The ground-glass opacity areas seen on the 2019 CT are not identified. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or adenopathy is identified.

Heart: Cardiomegaly, cardiothoracic ratio is 0.56. Pericardial effusion with thickness up to 7 mm. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending and left circumflex, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Aortic and mitral valve calcifications.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. The left pulmonary artery is dilated up to 3.2 cm, the right up to 2.8 cm. Multiple calcifications of the aortic arch.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Osteoarthritis of the shoulder joints (no change).

Upper abdomen: Unremarkable.
val_impression:
1. Against the background of incomplete inspiration, respiratory artifacts, and gravitational changes, consolidation and ground-glass opacity of the left lung may be a consequence of an unexpanded lung or, less likely, reflect inflammatory changes.

2. Multiple calcified nodules are likely healed granulomas and require no follow-up.

3. Coronary artery calcifications are identified in the left anterior descending and left circumflex, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for consideration of preventive risk modification or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

4. Mild cardiomegaly. Small pericardial effusion. Aortic and mitral valve calcifications. Thoracic aorta calcifications.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
